perturbation.original_text: The initial production period shall be for a period of two (2) months to commence on November 4, 1998 (the "Production Period"), and the remaining term of this Agreement shall be for a period of twenty four (24) months to commence on the tentative launch date of January 4, 1999, unless terminated earlier as provided herein (the "Promotion Period"), (The Production Period and the Promotion Period shall be collectively referred to as the "Initial Term").
perturbation.changed_text: The initial production period shall be for a period of two (2) months to commence on November 4, 1998 (the "Preparation Phase"), and the remaining term of this Agreement shall be for a period of twenty four (24) months to commence on the tentative launch date of January 4, 1999, unless terminated earlier as provided herein (the "Marketing Campaign"), (The Preparation Phase and the Marketing Campaign shall be collectively referred to as the "Initial Term").
perturbation.explanation: By changing 'Production Period' to 'Preparation Phase' and 'Promotion Period' to 'Marketing Campaign,' and not updating subsequent references, the contract becomes internally inconsistent. Later references to 'Production Period' and 'Promotion Period' now lack clear definition, creating ambiguity. For example, section 2.A and 2.C now refers to 'Production Period' and 'Promotion Period', respectively which is contradictory as it is not being reffered to a 'Preparation Phase' or 'Marketing Campaign' that was modified in Section 1.A.
perturbation.contradicted_text: During the Production Period, iVillage will design, develop, construct and host a Ford bridge site (the "Bridge Site") which shall include approximately [*] pages of content and other interactive material such as a travel planner or a car design feature. During the Promotion Period, iVillage will continue to host, maintain and update the Bridge Site. Upon receipt from iVillage of the proposed Bridge Site design and content, FMM shall have no more than five (5) business days in which to provide iVillage with its acceptance or rejection of the design and content. If iVillage does not receive FMM's acceptance or rejection of such within the allotted time, iVillage shall deem FMM's silence as acceptance. The Parties shall work together to determine the content mix and delivery deadlines in order to maximize the effectiveness of the sponsorship campaign.
perturbation.justification: The changed text renames 'Production Period' to 'Preparation Phase' and 'Promotion Period' to 'Marketing Campaign'. The contradicted text refers to 'Production Period' and 'Promotion Period' for specific obligations regarding website design, content, and hosting. Because the terms were changed and never updated in the contradicted text (which expects those terms), there's a clear contradiction. The altered definitions create ambiguity about the time frame and specific actions required by each party (e.g., iVillage's design obligations during the 'Production Period'). This aligns with the 'Misaligned Terminology - In Text Contradiction' definition, where changing terms without updating references causes conflicting obligations and uncertainty.

perturbation.original_text: During the Initial Term, iVillage will design, create and deliver [*] Ford-branded advertising units. The advertising units shall be subject to FMM's final approval. iVillage will deliver approximately [*] new advertising units each during the Promotion Period.
perturbation.changed_text: During the Initial Term, iVillage will design, create and deliver [*] Ford-branded advertising units. These marketing elements shall be subject to FMM's final approval. iVillage will deliver approximately [*] new advertising units each during the Marketing Phase.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'advertising units' to 'marketing elements' introduces inconsistency. It's unclear if these are synonymous. Later references to 'advertising units', such as in Section 7, now have an ambiguous referent. The contradiction occurs because the meaning of 'advertising units' is altered, thereby impacting how proprietary rights are assigned later in the contract specifically in Section 7, were it is being reffered to 'advertising units'.
perturbation.contradicted_text: Upon execution and delivery of this Agreement, iVillage assigns to FMC all right, title and interest in and to the content, design and intellectual property, rights created specifically for and unique to the Bridge Site, advertising units, and other promotional elements set forth in this Agreement (collectively, the "Materials"). Notwithstanding the foregoing, iVillage expressly retains all right, title and interest in and to the programs and software that are used in connection with the creation and operation of, but are not created specifically, for and unique to the Materials (the "iVillage Proprietary Materials"). FMC acknowledges and agrees that the iVillage Proprietary Materials are used by iVillage in creating and developing Web sites for itself and other parties. FMC further acknowledges and agrees that iVillage will be using certain licensed programs and software owned by third parties for portions of the development and creation of the Materials and that FMC will not acquire any right in or to those copyrighted materials. iVillage agrees to execute any and all necessary further documents that FMC may reasonably request to fully vest the intellectual property rights related to the Materials in FMC and, if requested, to reasonable assist FMC in registering such rights in the name of FMC.
perturbation.justification: The change from 'advertising units' to 'marketing elements' introduces ambiguity and inconsistency. The contradicted text (Section 7) assigns rights based on 'advertising units'. If 'marketing elements' is not synonymous with 'advertising units', the scope of assigned intellectual property rights becomes unclear, potentially leading to disputes over ownership and usage. This creates a direct conflict regarding which elements fall under the IP assignment clause, impacting enforceability.

perturbation.original_text: The advertising units of Ford Division and other Ford Motor Company entities shall be served by a third party advertisement server, which shall be compliant with Net gravity, or Doubleclick or other compatible technology.
perturbation.changed_text: The promotional materials of Ford Division and other Ford Motor Company entities shall be served by a third party advertisement server, which shall be compliant with Net gravity, or Doubleclick or other compatible technology.
perturbation.explanation: By changing the original text 'advertising units' to 'promotional materials' this introduces ambiguity. In section 2.C (iii) the initial wording specifies that if the 'advertising impressions' are not met, there are options like extending the term or refunding money. Now, because 'advertising units' has been replaced with 'promotional materials', it creates uncertainty, as this term wasn't previously specified as related to 'advertising impressions' specifically. This is contradictory because the consequences for not meeting impressions are specified based on 'advertising impressions', not broadly defined promotional materials.
perturbation.contradicted_text: In the event that iVillage fails to deliver the advertising impressions during the Promotion Period, FMM shall have the option of either (a) extending the Initial Term of this Agreement for an additional three (3) month period to "make good" the undelivered impressions or (b) requiring iVillage to refund to FMM an amount equal to [*] for each [*] impressions which were not delivered.
perturbation.justification: The change from 'advertising units' to 'promotional materials' introduces ambiguity regarding the scope of 'advertising impressions' which are specifically tied to remedies in the contradicted section. The contradicted section explicitly mentions consequences for failing to deliver 'advertising impressions'. By broadening the term to 'promotional materials', it creates uncertainty and a potential conflict in enforcement, since the term 'promotional materials' is not specifically tied to advertising impressions, making it unclear whether a failure to deliver other types of promotional materials triggers the remedies specified in the contradicted section. Therefore, this meets the definition of misaligned terminology leading to in-text contradiction because the contract uses multiple terms interchangeably without defining them, leading to conflicting obligations.
